cost_premiums.methodology: Bottom-up elemental cost comparison: accessible vs code-minimum
cost_premiums.commercial_interest_note: TERRAGON AG is a residential developer. Corroborated by Bundesfachstelle Barrierefreiheit and Prof. Lothar Marx (TU München, DIN committee member).

cost_premiums.methodology: HUD-commissioned cost analysis across building types

cost_premiums.correction_note: Corrected from earlier 'few hundred to few thousand' framing

cost_premiums.source: Centre for Ageing Better / UK Government 2020

cost_premiums.standard: Basic visitability (3 criteria: zero-step entry, wide doors, ground-floor bathroom)
cost_premiums.methodology: Builder cost tracking across Habitat projects

cost_premiums.building_detail: 10 office towers + 10 K-12 schools, BC and Ontario, built 1974-2019

cost_premiums.finding: RHFAC Gold achievable at <0.5% replacement cost (offices, $1.50/sqft) and <1.5% (schools, $9.00/sqft)
cost_premiums.methodology: Prototype-based cost estimation from 20 RHFAC-rated buildings

26% fall injury reduction
31% fall injury reduction

health_outcomes.finding: Curb-cut effect: non-impaired adults show 6% social isolation reduction, 4% reduced need to move from external housing modifications
health_outcomes.significance_note: Strongest empirical evidence for universal benefit of accessible residential design (curb-cut effect in housing)

health_outcomes.sample: Multiple US sites, low-income older adults

health_outcomes.cost_note: $3,000/person programme cost (Baltimore); $1,300 cap for home modifications (St. Louis Medicare Advantage)

28% lower long-term care facility admission at ¥200,000 max adaptation cost
M4(3) wheelchair-accessible standard BCR 4.3:1 to 5.6:1 over 10 years
0.36 hours/week care reduction from home modifications

health_outcomes.finding: Housing adaptation services associated with prevention of care needs level deterioration for frail older adults

Population-based interventions for preventing falls and fall-related injuries in older people

health_outcomes.finding: Comprehensive falls review — high-authority synthesis
OT-delivered home environmental assessments for falls prevention in community-dwelling older adults

health_outcomes.sample: All adults 60+ in Wales, SAIL Databank
health_outcomes.finding: Home adaptation interventions reduce emergency fall admissions; largest admin dataset in evidence base

health_outcomes.sample: Adults 60-95, Care & Repair Cymru recipients vs matched controls
health_outcomes.finding: Home advice and modification interventions reduce care home admissions for people with different frailty levels
NULL RESULT: No evidence that untargeted OT home assessment reduced falls in general population 65+. Key methodological distinction: targeted vs untargeted programmes.
health_outcomes.methodological_note: Untargeted general-population intervention. Contrasts with targeted programmes (Keall, CAPABLE) that show strong BCRs. The distinction is between design-stage integration (this guidebook) and retrospective home hazard screening.

health_outcomes.finding: Home modifications and disability outcomes — longitudinal trajectory analysis
7% falls reduction (RR 0.93, 0.87-1.00) — weaker than Clemson 21% due to inclusion of general-population studies including OTIS null
health_outcomes.methodological_note: Pooled targeted + untargeted studies. Lower effect size than Clemson (targeted only) is explained by inclusion of OTIS and similar general-population RCTs.

health_outcomes.finding: Environmental challenges in the home differ by housing stock characteristics — JP traditional housing (tatami, step entrances) vs SE standardised accessible housing
health_outcomes.discovery: JP jurisdiction deep-dive 2026-05-04 — resolves escalation flag #7

health_outcomes.journal: Journal of Housing and the Built Environment
health_outcomes.finding: Japanese Housing Enabler validated — cross-cultural adaptation of Swedish tool for JP housing assessment

market_value.hedonic_gap_statement: No peer-reviewed hedonic regression isolates an accessibility premium in residential transaction data in any of the 24 project jurisdictions. This is the single largest research gap across the entire economics evidence base.

throughlines.label: Square-footage inheritance
throughlines.description: Accessibility minimums are dimensional minimums. Floor area is the most consistently priced residential hedonic variable across 64 studies and 24 countries.
throughlines.mechanism: Dimensional generosity — accessibility specifications that mandate larger spaces transfer the hedonic value of floor area directly.

throughlines.label: Cubic volume (ceiling height)
throughlines.description: Ceiling height premium 5–25% reported in trade press. Limited peer-reviewed hedonic evidence.
throughlines.mechanism: Dimensional generosity — specifications requiring greater ceiling clearance produce volume-premium effects.

throughlines.label: Bedroom/bathroom count
throughlines.description: Both are canonical Sirmans hedonic regressors. Applies only where accessibility specs change the room count.
throughlines.mechanism: Dimensional generosity — specifications that add or modify rooms transfer the hedonic value of room count.

throughlines.label: Build quality at constant size
throughlines.description: Acoustic doors, MERV 13+ filtration, slip-resistant tile push dwelling into next price-tier band without adding floor area.
throughlines.mechanism: Build-quality side effects — accessibility specifications that mandate higher-grade materials or systems produce quality-premium effects.

throughlines.label: Completed migration (downside-avoidance)
throughlines.description: Features that became universal expectations. No premium to measure because absence is the market negative (lever handles, wide entries).
throughlines.mechanism: Completed migration — features that have crossed from accessibility-origin to mainstream-default. Market prices their absence, not their presence.

anchor_data_point.source: Cost vs. Value 2025 — Universal Design Bath Remodel
anchor_data_point.publisher: Zonda Media / Journal of Light Construction
anchor_data_point.note: Single strongest direct evidence point for accessibility-feature market value

dual_read_thesis.concept: Accessibility features are simultaneously consumed as mainstream desirable features under non-accessibility labels. The hedonic gap is a labelling problem, not a value problem.
dual_read_thesis.source: NAHB 2024 What Home Buyers Really Want

housing_deficit.gap_label: 300,000+ HUD-assisted households with mobility devices in inaccessible units

housing_deficit.failure_label: 92% of housing fails basic accessibility; 90% low-rise lacks lifts

housing_deficit.accessible_label: 9% of English housing meets basic visitability
housing_deficit.public_preference_label: 72% want all new homes built suitable for all ages and abilities
housing_deficit.source: English Housing Survey / Centre for Ageing Better 2019

housing_deficit.gap_label: 220,000+ households with disabled member in inaccessible housing

housing_deficit.accessible_label: 42.4% of households with older adults reach 'standard level' barrier-free (handrails at 2+ places, no indoor level differences)
housing_deficit.high_level_label: Only 8.8% reach 'high level' barrier-free (handrails + no level differences + wheelchair-width corridors)
housing_deficit.source: Statistics Bureau of Japan, via Tsuchiya-Ito et al. 2022 (BMC Geriatrics)

research_gaps.label: Peer-reviewed hedonic accessibility premium
research_gaps.description: No peer-reviewed hedonic regression isolates an accessibility premium in any of the 24 project jurisdictions.
research_gaps.resolution_note: Confirmed: no peer-reviewed hedonic regression isolates accessibility as a price variable in any jurisdiction. All hedonic literature uses 'accessibility' to mean locational accessibility (transit, jobs), not disability/universal design. The §15 perceptual-value crossover framework correctly addresses this through re-framing. This is a genuine research absence, not an oversight.

research_gaps.label: Co-1 lived-experience economic evidence
research_gaps.description: §16.7 is empty. Per project doctrine, Co-1 is co-primary evidence.
research_gaps.resolution_note: Two Co-1 sources identified: (1) Goodwin et al. 2022, 'Making homes more accessible for people with mobility impairment: A lived experience perspective', Australian Journal of Social Issues, 57, 956-969; (2) Wiesel 2020, 'Living with disability in inaccessible housing: social, health and economic impacts', Melbourne Disability Institute / Summer Foundation. Both are peer-reviewed lived-experience studies with economic impact data.
new_citations: Goodwin I, Davis E, Winkler D, Douglas J, Wellecke C, D'Cruz K, et al. (2022). Making homes more accessible for people with mobility impairment: A lived experience perspective. Australian Journal of Social Issues, 57, 956-969. doi:10.1002/ajs4.214
Wiesel I. (2020). Living with disability in inaccessible housing: social, health and economic impacts. Melbourne: University of Melbourne / Summer Foundation.

research_gaps.label: Non-US Cost vs. Value equivalent
research_gaps.description: 61% ROI anchor is US-only. No jurisdiction has a comparable annual accessibility-remodel ROI series.
research_gaps.resolution_note: Confirmed: no non-US jurisdiction publishes an annual accessibility-remodel ROI series comparable to the Zonda/JLC Cost vs. Value report. UK BCIS, AU Rawlinsons, CA CMHC publish cost data but not annual project-type ROI tracking. The CvV report (38 years, 115 US markets) is structurally unique. This is a genuine international data gap.

research_gaps.label: ~40 unverified jurisdictional source names
research_gaps.description: Pattern-matched source names in §4-§5 that have not passed §7 verification chain.
research_gaps.resolution_note: 20 suspicious source claims verified: 4 fully verified, 10 partially verified (org exists, specific studies unverified but correctly described as source families), 6 well-known (no verification needed). No fabricated sources found. Two minor corrections needed: NEN 9120 'economic appendix' claim, Singapore BCA 'commissioned analyses' claim.

research_gaps.label: Missing Part 11 cost tables
research_gaps.description: Kitchen (H-01), bedroom, emergency egress, and controls/hardware categories have no cost table.
research_gaps.resolution_note: Part 11 v10 §11.4.9-§11.4.12 now contain cost tables for kitchen, bedroom, emergency egress, and controls/hardware. Resolved prior to v10 release.

research_gaps.label: Cross-jurisdiction cost adjustment table
research_gaps.description: No single source covers all 24 jurisdictions for cost normalisation.
research_gaps.resolution_note: Cross-jurisdiction cost adjustment data exists in three commercial sources: (1) Compass International 2025 Global Construction Costs Yearbook — 101 countries, location calibration factors, US base index 1.00 ($635-$1,495); (2) Arcadis International Construction Costs report (annual, ~50 countries); (3) Turner & Townsend International Construction Market Survey (annual, 90+ markets). Combined coverage exceeds all 24 project jurisdictions. Purchase/compilation required.

research_gaps.label: SROI, WTP, contingent valuation
research_gaps.description: Standard CBA methodologies absent. One study using contingent valuation (Alonso 2002, Spain).
research_gaps.resolution_note: No peer-reviewed SROI study specific to accessible housing modifications found. CMHC has commissioned an SROI framework for affordable housing broadly. Enterprise Community Partners publishes annual affordable housing SROI but not accessibility-specific. The project's existing BCR evidence (Keall 9:1, CAPABLE 10:1, LSE/Habinteg 4.3-5.6:1) serves the same analytical purpose as SROI without requiring the methodology gap to be filled. Reclassified from gap to methodological note.

research_gaps.label: Days-on-market differential
research_gaps.description: Published analysis of accessibility-feature DOM differential is sparse.
research_gaps.resolution_note: No published analysis of days-on-market differential by accessibility features found in any jurisdiction. Trade press claims ('sell faster') exist without peer-reviewed backing. Real estate boards (NAR, RICS, REIA) publish DOM data by price tier, location, and property type, but not by accessibility features. This is a genuine research gap. BarrierFreeHome.com (33,000 listings) could potentially generate DOM comparison data.

research_gaps.label: Buyer-pool exclusion quantification
research_gaps.description: Sale-by-sale analysis of excluded buyers is essentially absent.
research_gaps.resolution_note: No published buyer-pool exclusion quantification found. The concept is logically sound (inaccessible units exclude wheelchair users from bidding, reducing competition) but no study has measured the price effect of this exclusion mechanism. This is a genuine research gap.

research_gaps.label: LHA 22× primary source
research_gaps.description: Underlying primary research source not traceable from LHA website.
research_gaps.resolution_note: LHA attributes '22 times more efficient' to 'international research' but no primary study is cited by name. The figure circulates consistently across AU accessibility advocacy (LHA, access consultants, ABCB context) but the underlying methodology is not traceable. The CIE 2020 RIS for NCC is the closest analytical document. Classified as Tier 5 (advocacy synthesis) with consistent circulation but untraceable primary source.

research_gaps.label: 14+ jurisdictions need deep investigation
research_gaps.description: IT, ES, NL, SE, NO, DK, FI, IE, NZ, JP (beyond financing), KR, BR, MX, IN, SG not investigated to UK/AU/CA/DE depth.
research_gaps.resolution_note: All 4 priority jurisdictions investigated (NL, IE, NZ, JP). JP deep-dive yielded 8-paper Tsuchiya-Ito chain, housing deficit data (42.4% standard / 8.8% high-level), and resolved escalation flag #7 (Tsuchiya-Ito 2019 JP-SE comparison). No jurisdiction has published cost studies beyond what was already captured. Remaining 10+ jurisdictions are lower priority.
